risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On completion of the baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)." Comment: The paper explicitly states participants were randomly assigned, and the study description indicates a randomized controlled trial. Although the exact method of sequence generation is not described, the report provides sufficient context to judge this as a proper random allocation rather than a clearly non-random method.

Summary: The paper states that participants were "randomly assigned to one of two workshops" after baseline measures, but it gives no description of how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is reported. Comment: Because random assignment is described without any explicit concealment method, foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out, so the risk of selection bias from allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On arriving for the workshops, participants were instructed to complete informed consent and baseline outcome measures... participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops... The child maltreatment reporting workshop... [versus] the ethnic cultural sensitivity in therapy workshop." Comment: Participants and personnel delivering the intervention could not have been blinded to workshop allocation because the interventions were clearly different face-to-face training workshops. The outcomes were immediately post-workshop knowledge/recognition/self-report measures, which could plausibly be influenced by participants’ awareness of the training they received and by facilitators’ knowledge of the assigned condition. Therefore, performance bias is high for the study overall.

Quote: "After completion of the workshops, participants were readministered the outcome measures in random order." and "the change in score from pre to posttest found in the child maltreatment reporting condition were less than what we would have expected, which perhaps was due to use of self-report measures with limited psychometric support" Comment: The outcomes were assessed by the participants themselves via self-report questionnaires/vignette ratings, so the participant is the assessor. The paper does not report participant blinding, and knowledge of workshop allocation could clearly influence responses to these subjective measures (recognition/intention to report and knowledge scales), making detection bias likely.

Quote: "One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment." and Table 2 reports outcomes for "N = 54". Comment: The study randomized 55 participants and only 1 was lost/excluded before posttest assessment, leaving 54 analyzed. This is essentially complete follow-up (~98%) with no indication that dropout was related to treatment assignment or outcome, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the results.
